request.question: The 1977 drama film The creature (Spanish: La criatura ) and the 1975 film The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) feature what relationship?
request.answer: between a beast (monster) and a woman

title: Human Desire
Beast of Gévaudan
La criatura
This Man Must Die
La Bête (film)
Beauty and the Beast (2014 film)
Beauty and the Beast (1946 film)
Zémire et Azor
Beauty and the Beast (1987 film)
The Wandering Beast
sentences: Human Desire is a 1954 black-and-white film noir directed by Fritz Lang, starring Glenn Ford.
It is loosely based on the novel "La Bête humaine", by Émile Zola.
The story had been filmed twice before: "La Bête humaine" (1938) directed by Jean Renoir and "Die Bestie im Menschen" starring Ilka Grüning (1920).

The Beast of Gévaudan (French: "La Bête du Gévaudan" ; ] , Occitan: "La Bèstia de Gavaudan" ) is the historical name associated with the man-eating gray wolf, dog or wolfdog which terrorized the former province of Gévaudan (modern-day "département" of Lozère and part of Haute-Loire), in the Margeride Mountains in south-central France between 1764 and 1767.
The attacks, which covered an area stretching 90 by , were said to have been committed by a beast or beasts that had formidable teeth and immense tails according to contemporary eyewitnesses.
The creature (Spanish: La criatura ) is a 1977 drama film, directed by Eloy de la Iglesia.
The plot follows a respectable bourgeois housewife who chooses the love for her black German Shepherd over her relationship with her husband.
"La criatura" is notable, like Walerian Borowczyk's "La Bête" (1975) and Nagisa Oshima's "Max, Mon Amour" (1986), for openly involving zoophilia, then a novelty in Spanish Cinema.
This Man Must Die (French: Que la bête meure ), American title The Beast Must Die, is a 1969 French and Italian thriller film directed by Claude Chabrol.
The story is based on a 1938 novel by Cecil Day-Lewis, writing as Nicholas Blake, "The Beast Must Die".
The film had a total of 1,092,910 admissions in France.
The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) is a 1975 X rated French erotic fantasy horror film written, edited, and directed by Walerian Borowczyk.
Although sometimes compared with "Beauty and the Beast", there are no parallels in the plot except that it features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman.
The film was noted for its explicit sexual content upon its initial release.
It has become a cult film.
Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 2014 Franco-German romantic fantasy film based on the traditional fairy tale of the same name by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve.
Written by Christophe Gans and Sandra Vo-Anh and directed by Gans, the film stars Léa Seydoux as Belle and Vincent Cassel as the Beast.
Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 1946 French romantic fantasy film directed by French poet and filmmaker Jean Cocteau.

Starring Josette Day as Belle and Jean Marais as the Beast, it is an adaptation of the 1757 story "Beauty and the Beast", written by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont and published as part of a fairy tale anthology.
Zémire et Azor ("Zémire and Azor") is an opéra comique, described as a "comédie-ballet mêlée de chants et de danses", in four acts by the Belgian composer André Grétry.
The French text was by Jean-François Marmontel based on "La Belle et la bête" ("Beauty and the Beast") by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont, and "Amour pour amour" by P. C. Nivelle de La Chaussé.
The opera includes the famous coloratura display piece "La Fauvette" in which the soprano imitates birdsong.

Beauty and the Beast (alternatively: Cannon Movie Tales: Beauty and the Beast in United States, Die Schöne und das Biest in West Germany, La Bella e la Bestia in Italy, La Belle et la Bête in France) is a 1987 American/Israeli musical film, part of the 1980 film series Cannon Movie Tales.
It is a contemporary version of the classic tale of Beauty and the Beast.
The movie was filmed entirely in Israel, and the taglines were: ""The monster they feared was the prince she loved"" and ""The classic fairy tale about seeing with your heart"".
The Wandering Beast (French:La bête errante) is a 1932 French drama film directed by Marco de Gastyne and starring Gabriel Gabrio, Maurice Maillot and Os-Ko-Mon.
The film is set in Alaska.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

The plot follows a respectable bourgeois housewife who chooses the love for her black German Shepherd over her relationship with her husband.  "La criatura" is notable, like Walerian Borowczyk's "La Bête" (1975) and Nagisa Oshima's "Max, Mon Amour" (1986), for openly involving zoophilia, then a novelty in Spanish Cinema.

La Bête (film) | The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) is a 1975 X rated French erotic fantasy horror film written, edited, and directed by Walerian Borowczyk.  Although sometimes compared with "Beauty and the Beast", there are no parallels in the plot except that it features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman.  The film was noted for its explicit sexual content upon its initial release.  It has become a cult film.

La Bête Humaine (film) | La Bête Humaine (English: The Human Beast and Judas Was a Woman) is a 1938 French film directed by Jean Renoir, with cinematography by Curt Courant.  The picture features Jean Gabin and Simone Simon, and is loosely based on the novel of the same name by Émile Zola.

The Wandering Beast | The Wandering Beast (French:La bête errante) is a 1932 French drama film directed by Marco de Gastyne and starring Gabriel Gabrio, Maurice Maillot and Os-Ko-Mon.  The film is set in Alaska.

Beauty and the Beast (1946 film) | Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 1946 French romantic fantasy film directed by French poet and filmmaker Jean Cocteau.  Starring Josette Day as Belle and Jean Marais as the Beast, it is an adaptation of the 1757 story "Beauty and the Beast", written by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont and published as part of a fairy tale anthology.

Beauty and the Beast (2014 film) | Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 2014 Franco-German romantic fantasy film based on the traditional fairy tale of the same name by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve.  Written by Christophe Gans and Sandra Vo-Anh and directed by Gans, the film stars Léa Seydoux as Belle and Vincent Cassel as the Beast.

Inju: The Beast in the Shadow | Inju: The Beast in the Shadow (French: Inju, la bête dans l'ombre) is a 2008 film by Barbet Schroeder.  The film stars Benoît Magimel and Lika Minamoto and was filmed on location in Tokyo.
hop1_titles: Inju: The Beast in the Shadow
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - La criatura (1977): passage [1] — "notable ... for openly involving zoophilia" (passage [1], "La criatura | The creature").
  - La Bête (1975): passage [2] — "features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman" (passage [2], "La Bête (film) | The Beast").
- Ambiguities:
  - "La Bête" has multiple similarly titled pages present in the passages: "La Bête (film)" (1975) and "La Bête Humaine" (1938). Do not assume which is intended beyond the 1975 Borowczyk film.
  - No alternate candidates for "La criatura" appear in the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity names as they appear in the passages: "zoophilia" (passage [1]) and "the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman" (passage [2]).
  - Precise missing fact needed to answer the question: the canonical phrasing of the relationship both films feature (e.g., confirmation that both explicitly depict "zoophilia" or an equivalent description such as "a sexual/romantic relationship between a human woman and an animal/beast").
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "La criatura" (1977) page — check lede/infobox and the "Plot" and "Themes" or "Reception" sections for the exact wording (search keywords: "La criatura zoophilia Eloy de la Iglesia plot").
  2) Open "La Bête (film)" (1975) page — check lede/infobox and "Plot" or "Themes" for the exact phrasing of the relationship (search keywords: "La Bête 1975 Borowczyk relationship beast woman plot").
  3) If needed, open "La Bête" disambiguation / "La Bête Humaine" page to confirm which "La Bête" is meant and rule out other similarly titled works (check lede/dates to confirm 1975 Borowczyk film).
  4) Check secondary sections ("Reception"/"Controversy") on both pages for explicit mentions of zoophilia or sexual/romantic relationship language.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and the exact shared relationship wording is confirmed.
response.hop2_query: What relationship do La criatura (1977) and La Bête (1975) feature?
hop2_docs: La criatura | The creature (Spanish: La criatura ) is a 1977 drama film, directed by Eloy de la Iglesia.  The plot follows a respectable bourgeois housewife who chooses the love for her black German Shepherd over her relationship with her husband.  "La criatura" is notable, like Walerian Borowczyk's "La Bête" (1975) and Nagisa Oshima's "Max, Mon Amour" (1986), for openly involving zoophilia, then a novelty in Spanish Cinema.

La Bête (film) | The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) is a 1975 X rated French erotic fantasy horror film written, edited, and directed by Walerian Borowczyk.  Although sometimes compared with "Beauty and the Beast", there are no parallels in the plot except that it features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman.  The film was noted for its explicit sexual content upon its initial release.  It has become a cult film.
La Belle et la Bête | La Belle et la Bête is the original French fairytale known in English as Beauty and the Beast

La Bête Humaine (film) | La Bête Humaine (English: The Human Beast and Judas Was a Woman) is a 1938 French film directed by Jean Renoir, with cinematography by Curt Courant.  The picture features Jean Gabin and Simone Simon, and is loosely based on the novel of the same name by Émile Zola.
La Bête à Maît' Belhomme | "La Bête à Maît' Belhomme" is a short story by French author Guy de Maupassant, published in 1885.
La Bête Noire (comics) | La Bête Noire is a comic book written by Croatian writer Darko Macan and illustrated by Serbian artist Milan Jovanović published in Novi Sad in 2002.

La bête lumineuse | La bête lumineuse (English: "The Shimmering Beast") is a 1982 Canadian documentary film directed by Pierre Perrault, about a group of hunters who gather annually to hunt moose near Maniwaki, Quebec.  It was screened in the Un Certain Regard section at the 1983 Cannes Film Festival.
response.summary_2: Both films depict sexual relationships between a woman and an animal (zoophilia). La criatura is a 1977 drama film directed by Eloy de la Iglesia whose plot follows a bourgeois housewife who chooses love for her black German Shepherd and is noted for openly involving zoophilia. La Bête (film) is a 1975 X‑rated French erotic fantasy horror film by Walerian Borowczyk that features the relationship between a beast and a woman.
